beats.title: Jaimini's Questions to Markandeya
beats.summary: The chapter begins with an auspicious invocation (mangala) praising Hari's lotus feet. It then introduces the dialogue between the sage Jaimini and the great sage Markandeya. Jaimini praises the Mahabharata as the supreme scripture encompassing dharma, artha, kama, and moksha, and asks Markandeya to explain several mysteries: why the sinless Janardana (Vishnu) took human birth as Vasudeva, why Draupadi became the wife of five Pandavas, why Baladeva performed a pilgrimage to cure brahminicide, and why the mighty sons of Draupadi died helplessly. Markandeya agrees to answer.

beats.title: The Test of the Apsaras
beats.summary: Markandeya narrates to Jaimini the story of how the sage Narada once visited Indra in the Nandana garden and, when asked to choose an apsara to dance, declared that the most excellent apsara is the one who can disturb the sage Durvasas in his penance. This sets up a test of spiritual power.

beats.title: The Curse of Vapus and the Lineage of Garuda
beats.summary: The chapter narrates the story of the apsara Vapus, who is cursed by a sage to become a bird for sixteen years after attempting to disturb his penance. It also introduces the lineage of Garuda and an encounter between the bird Kaṅka and a rakshasa on Mount Kailasa.

beats.title: Kandhara's Revenge and New Marriage
beats.summary: Kandhara, a bird (patagaraja), avenges his elder brother Kanka's death by killing the demon (rakshasa) who murdered him. After the battle, the demon's wife Madanika seeks refuge with Kandhara and becomes his wife. They have a daughter named Tarksha.

beats.title: The Tale of Tārkṣī and the Battlefield Eggs
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana narrates the story of Tārkṣī, a celestial nymph born from Menakā and Tārkṣa, who becomes pregnant and visits the Kurukshetra battlefield during the Mahabharata war. Her womb is pierced by an arrow from Arjuna, and she gives birth to four eggs. After the war, the sage Śamīka discovers the eggs and reflects on the nature of fate and death, citing examples from mythology.

beats.title: The Rescue and Growth of the Young Birds
beats.summary: The sage Markandeya continues his narration. The young birds, having been rescued and nurtured by the sage, grow rapidly and gain knowledge. After a month, they fly to the sun's chariot path, observe the earth and oceans, and return. They express gratitude to the sage for saving them from death and acknowledge him as their father and guru. The sage reflects on the power of effort and destiny.

beats.title: The Birds' Tale of Indra's Test
beats.summary: A sage (the listener) is told by birds that they were once human sons of the sage Sukṛṣa, but were cursed by the sage Vipulasvān to become birds. They recount how Indra, disguised as an old broken-winged bird, came to test their father's compassion, asking for human flesh. The father refused, condemning the cruelty, and the birds now seek guidance.

beats.title: The Test of Truthfulness
beats.summary: A sage (viprendra) tests his disciples by asking them to sacrifice their bodies to feed a hungry bird. The disciples refuse, citing the importance of preserving life and fulfilling debts to ancestors, gods, and humans. The sage curses them to become animals. He then offers his own body to the bird, who reveals himself as Indra testing the sage's commitment to truth. Indra blesses the sage with divine knowledge.

beats.title: The City of the Body and the Curse of the Sage
beats.summary: A sage (likely Markandeya) recounts a teaching about the body as a city with nine gates, ruled by the self (king), with mind and intellect as ministers, and enemies like desire, anger, greed, and delusion. The king must guard the gates (senses) to remain safe; if he opens them, enemies enter and destroy him. The sage also narrates a story of Indra granting boons, a father's curse, and eventual redemption through knowledge.

beats.title: The Birds of Vindhya
beats.summary: Jaimini, a disciple of Vyasa, visits the Vindhya mountain to meet the sons of Drona who were cursed to become birds. He finds them reciting scriptures and marvels at their wisdom despite their avian form. The sage Shamika explains that they are not ordinary birds but wise beings who attained liberation through knowledge.

beats.title: Jaimini's Quest for Knowledge
beats.summary: Jaimini approaches the sage Markandeya to resolve doubts about the Mahabharata, and is directed to the sons of Drona (birds) on the Vindhya mountain. The birds welcome Jaimini and agree to answer his questions.

beats.title: The Four Forms of Narayana and His Avataras
beats.summary: The chapter begins with a question about the fate of Draupadi's sons and the Pandavas, then transitions into a hymn to Narayana, describing his four forms (Vasudeva, Sankarshana, Pradyumna, Aniruddha) and their functions, including the avataras of Varaha and Narasimha. It explains the nature of the supreme being and the purpose of divine incarnations.

beats.title: The Fall of Indra and the Descent of the Gods
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the story of Vritra's birth from Tvashtr's anger after Indra killed his son, and the subsequent decline of Indra's power. It also narrates the Earth's plea to the gods for relief from the burden of demons born as kings, leading to the descent of divine portions.

beats.title: The Divine Origins of the Pandavas and Balarama's Pilgrimage
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the divine origins of the Pandavas and Draupadi, and Balarama's decision to go on a pilgrimage to avoid the Kurukshetra war. It explains how Indra's tejas was divided among the Pandavas, and how Draupadi became the wife of all five. Balarama, after considering his relationships with both sides, chooses to go on a pilgrimage rather than participate in the war.

beats.title: Balarama's Penance and the Story of Harishchandra
beats.summary: Balarama, while visiting a hermitage, kills a suta (bard) in anger, then realizes his sin and undertakes a twelve-year vow of penance, traveling along the Sarasvati river in reverse direction. The chapter also introduces King Harishchandra of the Treta Yuga as a righteous ruler.

beats.title: Harishchandra's encounter with Vishwamitra
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, while hunting, hears cries of women and goes to help. He encounters sage Vishwamitra performing penance, and a demon Vighnaraja possesses the king, causing him to speak arrogantly. Vishwamitra becomes angry, causing the Vedic sciences he was mastering to vanish. The king then humbly apologizes, explaining his royal duties of charity, protection, and warfare. Vishwamitra tests him by asking for a donation, and Harishchandra offers anything, including his kingdom, wealth, wife, son, or life.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal: The Demand for Dakshina
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having given away his entire kingdom as a gift to sage Vishwamitra, is now asked to also give the sacrificial fee (dakshina) for the Rajasuya yajna. Despite having nothing left, he agrees to pay within a month, and Vishwamitra demands immediate payment, threatening a curse. Harishchandra, with his wife and son, prepares to leave the kingdom.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Departure and Vishwamitra's Curse
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having promised a donation to sage Vishwamitra, asks for time as he currently has no wealth. Vishwamitra grants him leave, blessing his path. As the king departs with his wife and son, the citizens lament and plead for him to stay, praising his virtues. Vishwamitra, angered by the king's hesitation, harshly rebukes him and strikes the queen with a staff. The king continues silently. The gods Vishve then criticize Vishwamitra's cruelty, and Vishwamitra curses them to become humans, later modifying it so they will have no offspring.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal Continues
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana continues the story of King Harishchandra. After being cursed by sage Vishvamitra, the king, along with his wife Shaivya and son, arrives at Varanasi. Vishvamitra demands the promised dakshina for the Rajasuya sacrifice. Harishchandra, now penniless, contemplates selling himself into servitude to fulfill his vow. His wife offers to help.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Despair and Vishvamitra's Intervention
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having sold his wife and son to a Brahmin, falls unconscious from grief. His wife laments, and their son cries for food. Sage Vishvamitra arrives, revives the king, and urges him to fulfill his promise of giving the dakshina.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal: Selling His Wife
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, tormented by the sage Vishwamitra's demand for a sacrificial fee, decides to sell his wife and son to fulfill his promise. He offers his wife as a slave, and a brahmin buys her. The queen laments as she is taken away, and the young prince Rohitashva cries, clinging to his mother.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal: Sale of Family and Vishvamitra's Demand
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having sold his wife and son to a Brahmana, laments their fate. The Brahmana takes them away. Sage Vishvamitra arrives, demands the dakshina, and is angered by the insufficient amount from the sale. He curses the king and leaves. The king, in despair, calls for a servant to complete the remaining time. Then Dharma appears in the form of a Chandala.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal: Sold to a Chandala
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having completed a great sacrifice, is confronted by the sage Vishwamitra who demands the remaining dakshina. When Harishchandra hesitates, Vishwamitra conjures a fierce Chandala who offers to buy the king as a slave. Despite his reluctance, Harishchandra is forced to sell himself to the Chandala to fulfill his debt. The Chandala takes him to his city, where Harishchandra, now a slave, sings mournfully about his plight.

beats.title: Harishchandra in the Cremation Ground
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having lost his kingdom and family due to the sage Vishwamitra's curse, is forced to work as a servant for a chandala in a cremation ground. He laments his fate, remembering his wife and son, and describes the horrific scene of the cremation ground filled with ghosts, jackals, and burning corpses.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal in Hell and Rebirths
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, after being cursed by sage Vishvamitra, undergoes a series of horrific rebirths and experiences in hell, witnessing the consequences of his actions and the nature of karma.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ordeal and Awakening
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, after a dream-like experience of twelve years of suffering in various hellish realms, awakens and prays to the gods for protection. He continues his life as a servant in a cremation ground, still separated from his family, when a woman arrives carrying her dead son.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Lament at the Crematorium
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having lost his kingdom, lives in a crematorium. His wife Shaivya arrives with their son who died from a snakebite. They lament their misfortunes, recalling the king's past sacrifices and the loss of everything.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Lament at the Cremation Ground
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, having lost his kingdom, sold his family, and now his son dies from a snakebite. He and his wife lament at the cremation ground, where the queen recognizes him despite his degraded state as a chandala. Harishchandra explains his plight and considers entering fire without the chandala's permission.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Grief and Divine Intervention
beats.summary: King Harishchandra, overwhelmed by grief at the death of his son, prepares to enter the funeral pyre with his wife. The gods, led by Dharma and Indra, intervene to prevent his suicide, praising his virtues and offering boons.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Refusal of Heaven Without His Subjects
beats.summary: Indra invites King Harishchandra to heaven with his family, but Harishchandra refuses to abandon his subjects who are grieving for him. He argues that abandoning devotees is a great sin, and insists that his subjects share in his merit or he will not go to heaven. Indra explains that each person's karma is individual, but Harishchandra remains steadfast in his loyalty.

beats.title: Harishchandra's Ascension and Vashishtha's Anger
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana continues the story of King Harishchandra. After his ascension to heaven, the sage Vashishtha emerges from a twelve-year penance on the Ganges and learns of the events that transpired: Harishchandra's loss of kingdom, his association with a chandala, and the sale of his wife and son. Vashishtha becomes angry with Vishwamitra, who caused these events, contrasting this anger with his earlier restraint when Vishwamitra killed his hundred sons. He laments that the truthful, peaceful, and devoted king was reduced to such a state.

beats.title: The Battle of the Sages: Vasishtha and Vishvamitra as Birds
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the epic battle between the sages Vasishtha and Vishvamitra, who have been transformed into birds (a crane and a heron) due to a mutual curse. Their fight causes widespread destruction, prompting Brahma to intervene and reconcile them, revealing that their conflict was a result of King Harishchandra's Rajasuya sacrifice. The sages then make peace and return to their respective abodes.

beats.title: Sumati's Wisdom on the Cycle of Birth and Death
beats.summary: Jaimini asks the sage birds about the mysteries of birth, growth, existence in the womb, death, and the fruition of karma. The birds recount a story of a sage named Sumati who, when urged by his father to study the Vedas and follow the ashrama system, reveals that he has experienced countless lifetimes and knows the futility of such pursuits. Sumati describes the suffering in the womb and the endless cycle of births and deaths.

beats.title: The Son's Discourse on Transmigration and Liberation
beats.summary: A son recounts to his father his past lives and the suffering inherent in the cycle of birth and death, culminating in the realization that true knowledge (jñāna) leads to liberation (mokṣa). He describes his previous birth as a wise brāhmaṇa who, through yoga and association with the wise, attained self-knowledge but fell due to negligence, yet retained memory across births. Now determined to transcend saṃsāra, he rejects ritualistic religion and seeks the supreme Brahman beyond all dualities.

beats.title: The Son's Discourse on Death and Afterlife
beats.summary: A son describes the process of death and the afterlife to his father, explaining how one's actions determine the quality of death and the subsequent journey to Yama's realm.

beats.title: The Fate of the Sinful After Death
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the post-death journey of a sinful person. It details how Yama's messengers bind the soul and drag it through a harsh path to Yama's city, the torments of the Raurava hell, and the eventual rebirth in lower life forms or deformed human births. It also mentions the role of offerings by relatives in alleviating suffering.

beats.title: The Process of Rebirth and Fetal Development
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the process of rebirth and fetal development. It explains how beings fall into hells or ascend to heavens based on their karma, then take birth in various wombs. The text details the formation of the embryo, the growth of limbs, and the fetus's experiences in the womb, including its memories of past lives and resolve to avoid future births.

beats.title: The Dialogue on Samsara and Hell
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana describes the cycle of birth, death, and rebirth (samsara) as a painful wheel, comparing it to a waterwheel. It details the sufferings in the womb, at birth, in childhood, youth, old age, and death, as well as the torments of hells like Raurava and Maharaurava. The father praises the son's exposition and asks for more details about the hells.

beats.title: Description of Hells
beats.summary: This chapter describes various hells (narakas) where sinners suffer for their misdeeds. It details the torments in Raurava, Tamisra, Nikrintana, Apratishtha, and Asipatravana hells, emphasizing the karmic consequences of evil actions.

beats.title: The Torments of Hell and the King's Question
beats.summary: The chapter describes the torments of hell, specifically the Asipatravana (sword-leaf forest) and Taptakumbha (heated cauldron) hells, as narrated by a father to his son. A king named Vipashcit, who performed righteous deeds, questions why he is suffering in hell, and a Yama servant explains his past sin of obstructing cows from drinking water.

beats.title: The King's Inquiry into Hellish Punishments
beats.summary: The chapter is a dialogue between a king (the son) and a Yama messenger (Yamakiṅkara) in the Markandeya Purana. The king, who has died and is experiencing hellish torments, asks about the punishments for various sins. The messenger explains that even a small sin, such as neglecting the proper season for intercourse with one's wife, can lead to severe suffering. The king inquires about specific tortures he witnesses, and the messenger begins to explain the karmic consequences.

beats.title: The Exhaustion of Karma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Markandeya Purana discusses the nature of karma, explaining how both merit (puṇya) and demerit (pāpa) are exhausted through enjoyment or suffering. It details how even small actions can lead to significant consequences, and describes the specific punishments for sins like looking at others' wives with evil intent.
